warnings: Financial statements for 2025 are audited by Kezos & Dunlavy LLC; 2024 and 2023 are audited by Reese CPA LLC — two different auditors across the three-year period.
Special Risk #4 explicitly flags that the franchisor's financial condition calls into question its ability to provide services and support.
Brand Development Fund ended 2025 with a negative balance of -$109,274.44, indicating overspending relative to collections.
Members' equity was negative (deficit) at start of 2025 (-$360,693) and only turned slightly positive ($58,787) after a strong 2025 net income year.
Franchise agreement and exhibits are included in the document but per extraction rules, fees and costs are drawn only from Items 1-23 and financial statements.

extracted.brandBackground: VaVia is a dumpster rental, waste removal, and waste disposal franchise founded in 2018 and began franchising in March 2019, operating primarily in the Southeast U.S.
extracted.conceptRationale: VaVia provides dumpster rentals and waste removal services to residential and commercial customers, a field-service/trade-services model with no retail storefront.
extracted.staffingRationale: Operations require drivers/haulers and a managing owner or approved manager, but the back-office can be home-based and the model is described as owner-operated with a small crew.

leadership.name: Tim Wells
leadership.background: CEO since April 2020; previously held VP-level roles at Waste Management in Franklin, TN from 1998–2019.
leadership.whyItMatters: Deep industry operating experience at the largest waste company in North America.

leadership.name: Phillip C. Nappi
leadership.background: Co-founder; President and CFO since September 2018.
leadership.whyItMatters: Leads financial management and oversees initial training as primary trainer.

leadership.name: Michael M. Ampe
leadership.role: Chairman of the Board, Vice President, and Chief Technology Officer
leadership.background: Co-founder; Chairman, VP, and CTO since September 2018.
leadership.whyItMatters: Responsible for technology platform and system development.

leadership.name: John G. Crawford
leadership.role: Director, Vice President, Secretary, and Chief Strategy Officer
leadership.background: Co-founder; Director, VP, Secretary, and CSO since September 2018.
leadership.whyItMatters: Shapes long-term growth and franchise system strategy.

leadership.name: Eric Gustafsson
leadership.background: VP of Franchise Development since September 2025; previously VP of Franchise Development at Won Life Brands.
leadership.whyItMatters: Drives franchisee recruitment and system expansion.

leadership.name: Allison Pulk
leadership.background: Director of Operations since February 2023; previously Office Manager for VaVia Nashville franchise.
leadership.whyItMatters: Oversees day-to-day operational standards across the franchise network.

item19.notes: Item 19 discloses four tables: (1) Company Owned Outlet P&L for 2025 (Nashville, TN — 6 territories, ~1.87M population); (2) Operational Franchise Outlet summary descriptions (16 outlets); (3) Gross Sales, containers, hauls, and disposal fees for 16 Operational Franchise Outlets in 2025; (4) Dumpster inventory growth over first 8 quarters for all 16 outlets. No separate EBITDA or net-income table is disclosed for franchised outlets. The company-owned outlet table includes an Adjusted Profit figure. Data is not GAAP-prepared and is unaudited for franchisee outlets.

cohorts.label: Company Owned Outlet – Nashville, TN (Gross Sales)
cohorts.basis: 2025 Calendar Year actual gross sales for the single company-owned outlet operating across ~6 territories (1.87M population), per Item 19 Table 1.

cohorts.label: Company Owned Outlet – Nashville, TN (Adjusted Profit)
cohorts.basis: 2025 Adjusted Profit = Gross Margin less Disclosed Operating Expenses and Adjusted Franchise-Related Fees (royalty 8%, BDF 1%, local marketing 1.5%); not equal to net profit per Item 19 Table 1 notes.

cohorts.label: Operational Franchise Outlets – Average Gross Sales (2025)
cohorts.basis: Average of 16 Operational Franchise Outlets for 2025 Calendar Year per Item 19 Table 3; 7 above average, 9 below.

cohorts.label: Operational Franchise Outlets – Median Gross Sales (2025)
cohorts.basis: Median gross sales across 16 Operational Franchise Outlets for 2025 per Item 19 Table 3.

cohorts.label: Dumpster Inventory Growth – Average Containers at Q1
cohorts.basis: Average container count at end of Q1 across 16 outlets = 46 per Item 19 Table 4.

cohorts.label: Dumpster Inventory Growth – Average Containers at Q8
cohorts.basis: Average container count at end of Q8 across 16 outlets = 147 per Item 19 Table 4.

lineItems.notes: Single territory; paid to franchisor at signing. Item 7, p.12.
Build-out of Operations Center (non-retail back-office). Item 7, p.12.

lineItems.category: Waste Containers and Equipment – Initial Dumpster Inventory
lineItems.notes: 40–50 dumpsters required at opening; $3,250–$5,650 per unit. Item 7, p.12.

lineItems.category: Waste Containers and Equipment – Supplemental Dumpster Inventory
lineItems.notes: 50–70 additional dumpsters recommended within first year. Item 7, p.12.

lineItems.category: Waste Container Freight and Sales Tax
lineItems.notes: Freight and tax on dumpster purchases. Item 7, p.12.

lineItems.category: Service Vehicle with Vehicle Wrap Lease
lineItems.notes: Low = 3 months lease payments for 2 vehicles; high = purchase of 2 new vehicles. Item 7, p.12.

lineItems.category: Vehicle Freight, Registration and Sales Tax
lineItems.notes: One-time costs to ready vehicles for service. Item 7, p.12.

lineItems.category: Office Furniture, Signs, Supplies and Uniforms
lineItems.notes: Initial inventory of desks, chairs, uniforms, and supplies. Item 7, p.12.

lineItems.category: Computer, Software, and Business Management System
lineItems.notes: Desktop computer, mobile device, and initial BMS license. Item 7, p.12.
Minimum spend prior to and during first 3 months. Item 7, p.12.
Estimated 3 months of Operations Center lease payments (deposit assumed = 2 months rent). Item 7, p.13.
Initial deposit equal to 3 months of insurance premiums. Item 7, p.13.
Travel and lodging to attend pre-opening training in Nashville, TN. Item 7, p.13.
Attorneys, accountants, and advisors for start-up. Item 7, p.13.
Varies by local, county, and state regulations. Item 7, p.13.
Working capital for first 3 months (salaries, inventory, rent, utilities); excludes owner compensation. Item 7, p.13.

flatMonthlyFees.name: Business Management Software System Monthly License Fee

hiddenCosts.name: Out-of-Territory Royalty Rate
hiddenCosts.description: Royalty rate increases to 12% of Gross Sales for Out of Territory Customers above the 10% Out of Territory Threshold.

hiddenCosts.name: Annual Conference Attendance Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Up to $1,000 per year for mandatory annual system conference attendance.

hiddenCosts.name: Additional Employee Initial Training Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Currently $500 per person per day for individuals beyond the two included in initial training.

hiddenCosts.name: Supplemental Training Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Currently $500 per trainer per day plus travel expenses if franchisor requires or franchisee requests additional training.

hiddenCosts.name: Non-Compliance: Customer Reviews Fee
hiddenCosts.description: $250–$750 per month if aggregate customer review rating falls below 4.5 stars or 90% approval.

hiddenCosts.name: Reporting Non-Compliance Fee
hiddenCosts.description: $500 per occurrence for failure to timely submit royalty and activity reports.

hiddenCosts.name: Operations Non-Compliance Fee
hiddenCosts.description: $450–$1,000 per occurrence for failure to comply with operational standards, plus inspection/re-inspection costs.

hiddenCosts.name: Payment Non-Compliance Fee
hiddenCosts.description: $150 per occurrence for failure to timely pay fees, plus interest, costs, and legal fees.

hiddenCosts.name: Interest on Overdue Amounts
hiddenCosts.description: 18% per annum on all overdue fees and payments.

hiddenCosts.name: NSF / Failed EFT Fee
hiddenCosts.description: 5% of amount or $30 (whichever is greater) for insufficient funds or failed electronic transfers.

hiddenCosts.name: Audit Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Costs of financial audit if underreporting of 2% or greater is found, including audit, legal, travel, and accommodations.

hiddenCosts.name: Quality Assurance Audit Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Reimbursement of actual costs if franchisor engages a third party for periodic QA audits or mystery shopper programs.

hiddenCosts.name: Supplier and Product Review Fee
hiddenCosts.description: $250 per supplier or item plus franchisor's costs to evaluate a proposed new supplier.

hiddenCosts.name: Transfer Fee
hiddenCosts.description: $20,000 upon approved transfer of the franchise.

hiddenCosts.name: Renewal Fee
hiddenCosts.description: 25% of the then-current initial franchise fee per territory being renewed.

hiddenCosts.name: Computer System Maintenance and Repair
hiddenCosts.description: Estimated $1,000–$2,000 per year for maintenance, repair, and updates to required computer systems.

hiddenCosts.name: Credit Card Processing Fees
hiddenCosts.description: Percentage-based fee on all credit card processed Gross Sales; franchisee must use designated processor.

hiddenCosts.name: Opening Assistance Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Reimbursement of franchisor's travel, lodging, and meals for up to 3 days of on-site opening assistance if requested.

hiddenCosts.name: Customer Service and Refunds
hiddenCosts.description: Franchisee must reimburse franchisor for any customer refunds or credits paid on franchisee's behalf.

hiddenCosts.name: Local and Regional Advertising Cooperative Contributions
hiddenCosts.description: Up to 2% of monthly Gross Sales if franchisor establishes a cooperative in franchisee's market; currently not assessed.

rentDetail.source: Item 7, p.13 — lease payments estimated at $3,000–$6,000 for 3 months; Operations Center typically ~750 sq ft office plus half-acre lot in industrial park.

operationalRisks.title: Franchisor Financial Condition Risk
operationalRisks.description: The FDD's Special Risks section explicitly states that the franchisor's financial condition calls into question its ability to provide services and support; members' equity was negative through end of 2024.
operationalRisks.source: Special Risks, p.iv; Item 21 / Exhibit D financial statements

operationalRisks.title: Mandatory Minimum Royalty Payments
operationalRisks.description: Franchisees must pay minimum monthly royalties regardless of sales levels (e.g., $1,650/month after month 12 for 1 territory), and failure to pay may result in termination.

operationalRisks.title: Spousal Liability Guarantee
operationalRisks.description: Each owner's spouse must personally guarantee all financial obligations under the franchise agreement, placing marital and personal assets at risk.

operationalRisks.title: Out-of-State Dispute Resolution
operationalRisks.description: All disputes must be mediated and arbitrated in Davidson County, Tennessee, which may be costly and disadvantageous for out-of-state franchisees.

operationalRisks.title: Non-Exclusive Territory
operationalRisks.description: Franchisees do not receive an exclusive territory; franchisor retains broad reserved rights including competing via alternative channels within the franchisee's territory.

operationalRisks.title: High Capital Requirement for Dumpster Inventory
operationalRisks.description: Franchisees must purchase 40–50 dumpsters at opening ($128K–$178K) and are recommended to add 50–70 more within year one ($200K–$280K), creating significant upfront capital exposure.

operationalRisks.title: Brand Development Fund Deficit
operationalRisks.description: The Brand Development Fund ended 2025 with a negative balance of -$109,274.44, indicating the fund spent more than it collected and may require higher future contributions.

operationalRisks.title: Franchisor Can Unilaterally Change System and Manuals
operationalRisks.description: Franchisor may modify the operations manual and system requirements without franchisee consent, potentially requiring additional investments.

operationalRisks.title: Termination for Non-Curable Defaults
operationalRisks.description: Multiple categories of non-curable defaults (e.g., three prior curable defaults, abandonment, insolvency) allow immediate termination with no cure period.

operationalRisks.title: Post-Termination Non-Compete
operationalRisks.description: 24-month non-compete covering the franchisee's operating territory plus a 50-mile radius and all other VaVia territories after termination or expiration.

operationalRisks.title: Renewal Requires Signing New Agreement
operationalRisks.description: Renewal requires signing the then-current franchise agreement, which may contain materially different terms, plus paying a renewal fee of 25% of the then-current initial franchise fee.

operationalRisks.title: Concentration of Royalty Revenue Risk
operationalRisks.description: In 2024, five franchisee groups accounted for 65% of total franchise royalty revenue, indicating high revenue concentration risk for the franchisor.
operationalRisks.source: Item 21 / Exhibit D, 2024 financial statements notes

financialCondition.auditorName: Kezos & Dunlavy, LLC (2025); Reese CPA LLC (2024, 2023)

riskReasons: 4 high-severity operational tripwires disclosed — see the tripwires section.
Franchisor financial distress disclosed (negative net worth) — confirm its ability to support the system against the audited statements.
notes: Average rent not found; fixed costs may be understated.

underwriting.assessment: With $250,000 liquid against a $647,021 mid-point build-out, there is a $397,021 capital gap. An SBA loan or an equity partner is required to do this deal. At mid-cohort revenue, modeled cash flow after debt service is about $97,747/mo before payroll and maintenance — a thin margin of error.

insights.conceptRationale: VaVia provides dumpster rentals and waste removal services to residential and commercial customers, a field-service/trade-services model with no retail storefront.

benchmark.label: Home & trade services (mobile / low-overhead)
benchmark.dominantRisk: Crew utilization, callback/warranty cost, and customer-acquisition cost. Low fixed overhead, but CAC and scheduling efficiency decide profitability.
benchmark.rampNote: 3–9 months to a steady job pipeline.
considerations: Budget materials/subcontractor COGS ~25–40% and field labor ~20–35%.
CAC (lead cost per booked job) is the dominant hidden line — ask Item 20 operators what they actually spend per job.
Confirm territory size, lead-generation expectations, and any required call-center/marketing fees.

crossCheck.message: No operating-EBITDA margin is disclosed in Item 19. The 10–25% band below is an industry estimate for this concept — validate it against real franchisee P&Ls (Item 20) before relying on any margin.

insights.staffingNote: Flagged as an lightly staffed model from the FDD, so labor is adjusted down to 10–21% — the 20–35% category band assumes a staffed venue. Confirm actual staffing with Item 20 franchisees.

buildup.label: − Occupancy / rent (not disclosed)
buildup.note: Item 7 disclosed no rent figure — benchmark occupancy applied so the line above isn't overstated

buildup.label: − Labor
buildup.note: lightly staffed · ≈ 3.4–7.2 FTE at ~$20/hr fully loaded

assumptions.detail: Classified as “Home & trade services (mobile / low-overhead)” from the FDD (Item 1 / brand description).
Read as lightly staffed from the FDD, which lowers the labor band applied below.
Item 7 disclosed no rent — occupancy estimated at the 2–6% category band.
Estimated at the 25–40% category band — COGS is never disclosed in an FDD.
Estimated at the 10–21% lightly staffed band — labor is never disclosed in an FDD.
Utilities, insurance, and R&M estimated at 6–10% (category catch-all).
Our calculation — the disclosed top line minus the benchmarked costs above. Not a figure stated in the FDD.

insights.consultCtaPitch: These are category benchmarks applied to the disclosed top line — your unit's real costs will differ, and that difference is the conversation. Want them pressure-tested against actual franchisee P&Ls for your territory?
insights.asOf: 2026 — general industry benchmarks, refine against real franchisee P&Ls
insights.disclaimer: Industry benchmark ranges for your own budgeting and Item 20 questions — NOT a projection of this franchise's results, and not investment advice.

financialCondition.headline: The franchisor flagged its own financial condition as a risk. The figures we extracted look comparatively stable, so confirm directly against the most recent statements.
financialCondition.context: Worth perspective: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt; revenue is growing; positive net worth; the business is profitable. Early-stage franchisors commonly run losses and carry a deficit while investing to scale — this reads more like spending ahead of revenue than a failing business. The real question is runway: how the gap is funded, and for how long.
body: What we found (2025-12-31, audited by Kezos & Dunlavy, LLC (2025); Reese CPA LLC (2024, 2023)): Net income of $1.20M on $3.05M of revenue — roughly 0.6x spent per $1 earned. Positive net worth of $59K. Cash of $1.11M against $321K of obligations due within a year (a current ratio of 6.67). Note: about 90% of those liabilities is deferred revenue — prepaid franchise fees the company owes as future services, not borrowed money. Netting it out, liabilities are still about 0.1x assets.
Why it matters to you: field support, training, technology, and marketing come out of the franchisor's budget. If outside funding slows, those are the first things to get cut.

Verify before you commit: ask for the most recent interim (unaudited) financials and the current-year funding plan; ask whether the parent will sign a guarantee of performance; and cross-check Item 20 — unit closures, transfers, and terminations are the operational tell that corroborates financial stress.
mitigants: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt
financialCondition.evidenceNote: Flagged on the FDD's "Special Risks" page and grounded in the Item 21 / Exhibit F audited financials. Figures are extracted; all ratios and grading are computed. Confirm against the source statements and a CPA before relying on them.
